agent1_snippets.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Mandibular Third Molars -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: Lower anterior crowding is not an indication of extraction of lower third molars. Crowding of the lower anterior teeth is sometimes observed simultaneously with the eruption of lower wisdom teeth. This has led to the belief that the erupting third molars cause a mesial migration of teeth, consequently moving the incisors, resulting in crowding. [11] Several studies have investigated this hypothesis. Still, most have not found a cause-effect relationship between anterior crowding and the eruption of lower third molars. [11] Consequently, Zawawi & Melis (2014) argue that extraction of lower third molars does not alleviate anterior crowding and therefore does not justify the potential risk of damage to IAN. [11]

agent1_snippets.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Mandibular Third Molars -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: Mandibular third molars are the most frequently extracted teeth, accounting for 18% of dental extractions. [1] [2] Third molars typically develop around the age of 8 to 15 [3] and erupt between the ages of 17 to 22. [4] As a result of this delayed eruption, mandibular third molars are often impacted, with 17 to 69% presenting with some degree of impaction. [5] Extraction of third molars is further complicated by its anatomical proximity to the inferior alveolar nerve (IAN), the third branch of the fifth cranial nerve, which supplies sensory function to the lower cheek, chin, lip, tongue, gingivae, and teeth. [6]

agent1_snippets.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Teeth -- Indications -- Supernumerary Teeth
agent1_snippets.content: A supernumerary tooth is defined as a tooth that is additional to the normal series. They present most commonly in the anterior maxilla. Supernumeraries can cause a range of clinical issues, including failure of eruption or displacement of a permanent tooth, dental crowding, and pathology. [20] If retention of a supernumerary tooth is not considered beneficial, then it is often planned for removal.

agent1_snippets.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- Pathophysiology
agent1_snippets.content: Impacted teeth characteristically remain under the gingival tissue and do not erupt into their ideal position. Impacted teeth can be a significant cause of malocclusion, leading to the shifting of adjacent teeth. When impacted teeth do not erupt into the dental arch, additional space exists for adjacent teeth to shift into, which will serve as an obstacle for if and when the impacted tooth may erupt later. Impacted third molars are often experienced during adolescence or early adulthood and may cause interferences in occlusion if not extracted or exposed. [17]

agent1_snippets.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Mandibular Third Molars -- Preparation -- Winter's Lines (WAR)
agent1_snippets.content: A formal index of quantification of surgical complexity of extraction of lower third molars was suggested by Winter in 1926. [15] This method involves the use of three illusory lines. The first of these is a white line that is drawn across the occlusal surfaces of the lower molars and extends posteriorly above the unerupted third molar. The second line, amber, follows the bone level from the distal aspect of the lower third molar, extending to the interseptal bone of the first molar. The third line, red, is a line drawn perpendicular from the white line extending down to the anticipated point of application on the unerupted lower third molar. This is usually the mesial aspect of the CEJ, except for disto-angular third molars, where the application point is distal. (See figure 2.)

agent1_snippets.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Mandibular Third Molars -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: Indications for extraction of a lower third molar include recurrent episodes of pericoronitis, unrestorable caries, or caries extending into the pulp. Furthermore, a horizontal or mesioangular lower third molar causing disto-cervical caries in the lower second molar is indicated for extraction to facilitate restorative treatment in the lower second molar. In addition, extraction is also indicated in third molars with odontogenic cysts or tumors, those impeding surgery (such as in patients undergoing orthognathic surgery), or in the line of a mandibular fracture. agent1_snippets.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- Introduction -- Classification of Malocclusion
agent1_snippets.content: Class I malocclusion is further categorized into three types by Dewey. In Dewey type 1, the incisors are crowded, the canines are positioned labially, or both. [5] In Dewey type 2, the maxillary incisors protrude. In type 3, the anterior teeth occlude edge to edge, or there is a crossbite or both. [5] The Anderson classification further categorizes class I malocclusion into types 4 and 5. In type 4, there is a posterior crossbite, which could be unilateral or bilateral. Type 5 occlusion is a class I molar relationship with mesioversion of the permanent first molar due to the extraction of a second deciduous molar or premolar. [6]

agent1_snippets.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Unerupted Teeth -- Anatomy and Physiology
agent1_snippets.content: Tooth impaction may affect anyone, with incidence rates varying from 5.6% to 18.8%, and it tends to be more common in female individuals. The frequency of tooth impaction follows a pattern opposite to the typical chronological eruption sequence. Teeth that erupt later are more likely to be impacted due to insufficient arch space. The mandibular 3rd molar is the most commonly impacted tooth, followed by the maxillary 3rd molar, maxillary canine, mandibular premolars, maxillary premolars, and 2nd molar. The teeth with the lowest impaction rates are the mandibular incisors, 1st molars, and primary teeth. Impaction of these teeth is relatively rare and is typically associated with specific conditions, such as retained deciduous teeth or abnormalities like an odontoma.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
agent2_snippets.content: Melanomas of skin are malignant neoplasms that arise from melanocytes in the cutaneous tissue and represent a specific form of skin cancer. سرطان الشفة الخبيث, which translates to malignant lip cancer, is a distinct malignant tumor that originates in the lip and is classified as an oral cavity cancer. Although both conditions are cancers, they affect different anatomical sites and are not subtypes of one another; each requires separate diagnostic and therapeutic approaches tailored to its location and biological behavior. Understanding the differences between melanomas of skin and سرطان الشفة الخبيث is important for accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment planning, and effective patient care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
agent2_snippets.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION represent a collective group of perinatal problems that encompass short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. Short gestation, defined as delivery before full term, often results in low birth weight because the infant has had insufficient time for normal growth. Low birth weight, in turn, frequently reflects fetal growth retardation, a condition in which the fetus does not achieve its genetically expected size. These three entities are closely interrelated, each contributing to the broader spectrum of fetal growth and malnutrition disorders, and together they increase the risk of adverse neonatal outcomes. Effective maternal care that optimizes nutrition and prevents premature delivery is essential for reducing the incidence of these interconnected conditions.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2071
agent2_snippets.content: Pregnancy-Related Procedures comprise a range of interventions used to manage complex obstetric conditions and facilitate safe delivery. Among these, the surgical treatment of ectopic pregnancy and abdominal pregnancy represents critical operative approaches for addressing abnormal implantation of the embryo, thereby preventing life‑threatening complications for the mother. In addition, Vaginal Assisted Delivery is included as a procedural option that supports the progression of labor and assists in the birth of the infant when additional support is required. Together, these child procedures under the umbrella of Pregnancy-Related Procedures illustrate the spectrum of surgical and assisted techniques employed in modern obstetric care.
